text:
5. CTA: primary CTA is a command, not a button, for this audience when possible (Railway's `curl | sh`, Bun's install line) — dev-tool audiences trust an executable command line over a "Get Started" button. Secondary CTA = "View docs" / "GitHub" link, low visual weight.
6. README-as-landing-page structure (from Evil Martians' 100-page study + DEV.to 2026 README-best-practices piece): first two lines must answer "what is this / why care" — if a reader can't parse it in ~10 seconds they bounce. One screen, not a wall — split deep docs into /docs and link out (tenex-edge already does this correctly: docs/daemon-design.md, docs/fabric-architecture.md, docs/product-spec/). Working install/run command near the top, copy-pasteable. Avoid: emoji section headers (reads as templated/low-effort in 2026), animated SVG banners (2022 novelty, now a negative signal), front-loaded marketing prose before any code block. Badges are fine ONLY if they communicate something real (CI passing) — vanity badges read as padding to this audience. A code-block-as-hero (a terminal session or a minimal working example) is now often preferred over a headline+subhead pair for genuinely technical/infra tools — worth considering leading tenex-edge's README with a short "who → chat write → chat read" terminal transcript before any prose, given the existing README already does something similar in "Try it." [...] 6. README-as-landing-page structure (from Evil Martians' 100-page study + DEV.to 2026 README-best-practices piece): first two lines must answer "what is this / why care" — if a reader can't parse it in ~10 seconds they bounce. One screen, not a wall — split deep docs into /docs and link out (tenex-edge already does this correctly: docs/daemon-design.md, docs/fabric-architecture.md, docs/product-spec/). Working install/run command near the top, copy-pasteable. Avoid: emoji section headers (reads as templated/low-effort in 2026), animated SVG banners (2022 novelty, now a negative signal), front-loaded marketing prose before any code block. Badges are fine ONLY if they communicate something real (CI passing) — vanity badges read as padding to this audience. A code-block-as-hero (a terminal session or a minimal working example) is now often preferred over a headline+subhead pair for genuinely technical/infra tools — worth considering leading tenex-edge's README with a short "who → chat write → chat read" terminal transcript before any prose, given the existing README already does something similar in "Try it." [...] It also surfaced the key creative tension Fable will need to resolve: the **collision/clobbering** framing is the top-of-funnel *hook* (what devs actually feel and click on) — but the doctrine distrusts it as narrow. The recommended reconciliation: lead the *hook* with collision-prevention, let *proof + architecture* carry the durable-identity story underneath. And a strong structural steer: for this audience, consider a **terminal transcript as hero** over a headline/subhead pair, install-command-as-CTA, no emoji headers or vanity badges. [...] 3. Feature triplet formula: one word or 2-3 word fragment as the label (bold), one clause underneath as proof/mechanism, not benefit-restated. Labels are adjectives about the PRODUCT (Fast/Agentic/Collaborative), not the user's feelings. [...] 4. Social proof: curated single quotes placed adjacent to the relevant feature, not batched in a testimonial carousel; logos only if genuinely recognizable — otherwise skip (a fake-looking logo wall actively hurts credibility with this audience).
